questions.prompt: A developer closes your ticket with 'Not a bug, works on my machine.' What is the most effective next step?
options: Re-open it and write that the developer clearly did not test properly
Reply with the exact build, environment, and steps you used, plus an artifact that reproduces it
Escalate to the manager immediately so they overrule the developer
Drop it, since the developer knows the code better than you do
questions.explanation: Most 'works on mine' calls are a missing-context problem. Supplying exact build, environment, steps, and proof closes the gap so the developer can reproduce it without a standoff.

questions.prompt: Which bug comment is most likely to get acted on without a dispute?
options: Checkout is broken again, did anyone test this?
Search is useless, whoever wrote it never tested it
Checkout fails on build 4.2.1, Chrome 125, staging. Steps attached. Expected: order confirmed. Actual: 500 error. Video attached.
Please fix checkout, it does not work for me sometimes
questions.explanation: It carries build, environment, steps, expected vs actual, and proof. There is nothing to argue about and no missing information, so the developer can act immediately.

questions.prompt: You and a developer disagree on whether a defect is real, and progress has stalled. When and how should you escalate?
options: Escalate now, emphasizing how unprofessional the developer has been
Never escalate; disagreeing with a developer is not your place
Escalate once you have facts the developer disputes, presenting impact, frequency, and repro data rather than blame
Escalate only after the release ships and the bug reaches customers
questions.explanation: Escalate when facts are disputed and progress has stalled, and do it with data — impact, frequency, reproduction — not emotion or personal criticism.

questions.prompt: Why does 'assume good faith' lead to faster fixes when a developer disputes a bug?
options: Because developers are always right about their own code
Because it keeps the exchange focused on the system and the missing context instead of on the people
Because being agreeable means the bug will be prioritized higher
Because it avoids ever having to provide evidence
questions.explanation: Most disputes come from missing context, not bad intent. Treating it that way keeps the conversation about reproducing the defect rather than defending egos, which resolves it sooner.
